title: Espionage Gadgets & Equipment
description: The tradecraft hardware — surveillance devices, communication tools, identity equipment, and the specialized instruments of intelligence work, from agency-issue to improvised field solutions.

entries.result: A miniature audio transmitter — the size of a button — designed to be sewn into a jacket lining with a battery life of seventy-two hours.
A complete legend package: passport, driver's license, credit cards, employment history, and three years of verifiable digital footprint for a person who does not exist.
A one-time pad in a water-soluble format — messages written in a specific ink on a specific paper that dissolves in thirty seconds when exposed to water.
A camera disguised as a shirt button that captures still images on a pressure-trigger, storing up to two hundred images on an internal chip.
A dead drop container — magnetized, weather-sealed, the size of a bolt — that can be affixed to any metal surface and retrieved by someone who knows where to look.
A signal jammer in a modified power bank that blocks cellular and wifi in a fifteen-meter radius while appearing to charge a phone.
A lock bypass tool for a specific model of electronic lock used in government facilities in three countries — single-use, leaves no physical trace.
A voice changer attached inline with a phone that modifies pitch, cadence, and vocal timbre in real time, maintaining conversation naturalness.
A surveillance detection route guide for a specific city — handwritten, in the notation system used by a specific service — indicating counter-surveillance positions and flush-out checkpoints.
A concealment device built into the spine of a reference book — hollowed, with a magnetic closure — sized to hold a USB drive and three pages of documents.
A dental-carried radio receiver — a tooth filling with a bone-conduction speaker — allowing one-way communication with no visible earpiece.
A modified USB cable that installs a keylogger on any computer it connects to, appearing as a standard charging cable.
A portable document scanner the size of a pen that can scan an A4 page in three seconds by running it along the surface.
A facial recognition countermeasure — a specific combination of makeup and light-reflective material — designed to defeat standard CCTV analytical systems.
A diplomatic pouch seal, official, for a country whose pouches are not subject to inspection at this particular border crossing.
A biometric bypass device for fingerprint scanners that stores up to five prints and deploys them on a contact pad in the fingertip.
A miniature explosive charge shaped and colored to be indistinguishable from a specific variety of chocolate — designed to destroy a document safe.
A tracking device with a seventy-two-hour battery that attaches magnetically and transmits GPS coordinates at five-minute intervals to a receiver up to fifteen hundred kilometers away.
A contact poison applicator — a modified ring — that transfers a compound through skin contact sufficient to induce a medical event in four to six hours.
A surveillance camera loop device that captures thirty seconds of footage and replays it on an infinite loop when inserted into a camera's signal path.
A one-time encryption device — physical, mechanical — for encoding messages using a key that cannot be reversed without the key document.
A miniature directional microphone designed to be placed behind a picture frame, capturing audio in a room with the range and clarity needed for telephone-quality recording.
A forged academic credential — complete with transcripts, reference letters, and a verifiable entry in a university's database — for a specific field of study.
A communications device built into a wristwatch — satellite uplink, encrypted, twenty-second message burst — designed for denied-area exfiltration.
A sedative compound in a perfume bottle, aerosolized, that produces unconsciousness in ninety seconds at close range and clears from the bloodstream in six hours.
A concealed camera for document photography — disguised as a lighter — that captures full-page resolution images under ambient light.
A radio frequency scanner that identifies active surveillance transmitters in a space, displayed as a map on a phone screen, indistinguishable from a standard app.
A persona-maintenance package — social media accounts, email correspondence, utility bills, a lease — sustaining a second identity for ongoing operational use.
A portable voice stress analyzer that runs on a phone, monitoring a conversation for deception indicators in real time through an earpiece.
A magnetic door stop that prevents a door from being opened from outside while leaving no visible indication that it's been placed.
A delayed-destruction device for document pouches — thermite-based, triggered by unauthorized opening — that ensures contents are unrecoverable.
An IMSI catcher disguised as a portable hotspot that captures mobile device identifiers and call metadata from all phones within a hundred meters.
A covert entry kit in a glasses case: pick set, tension tools, bypass cards, and a small fiber optic scope for under-door reconnaissance.
A concealed recording device in a pen that captures audio for twelve hours on a charge, with voice activation to conserve storage.
A currency converter for a specific black-market exchange route, with current rates and contacts written in a notation system that appears to be a personal shopping list.
A concealment device built into a car's undercarriage — magnetic, weather-sealed, vibration-resistant — designed for cross-border document transfer.
A social engineering script for a specific target organization's helpdesk, incorporating current internal terminology and process language, designed to extract access credentials.
A portable spectrographic pen that identifies the ink and paper composition of documents, useful for detecting forgeries or matching document origin.
A disguised wireless access point that performs a man-in-the-middle attack on connections passing through it while appearing as a legitimate network.
A concealed compartment in a standard suitcase, below the wheel well, with a combination release that requires knowing the mechanism.
A liquid that, when applied to a document, reveals writing that was applied with a specific pre-treatment compound but is invisible under normal conditions.
A remote access trojan on a USB stick that establishes a persistent backdoor when plugged into any connected computer, designed to appear as a driver installation.
A set of surveillance log sheets with pre-filled entries showing routine activity for a location over the past six weeks, formatted to match a specific service's documentation standard.
A disguised phone that appears to be a specific model of civilian handset but operates on encrypted communications infrastructure, with a panic wipe triggered by entering a specific PIN.
A behavioral profiling report on a recruitment target — six months of surveillance, psychological assessment, identified pressure points — prepared by someone with professional training.
A miniature safe house access kit: two sets of keys, a floor plan, emergency protocols, cached supplies list, and a contact number that answers only during a specific window each day.
A technical exploitation guide for a specific model of hotel room safe — eleven pages, three methods, success rates by brand variant — written in the format of an internal training document.
A GPS spoofer that transmits a false location signal from a target device while the actual device transmits normally, creating a ghost track at a specified location.
A concealed injection device in a modified pen cap that delivers a compound subdermally on contact — designed for a single use.
A complete safe house establishment kit: lease agreement template, utility setup instructions, supply list, emergency protocols, and a cover business registration document.
A dual-use device that functions as a working calculator and, when a specific sequence is entered, opens an encrypted partition containing operational files.
A signal brush-pass device — a modified newspaper — with an internal compartment that allows a physical item to be passed between two people in apparent non-contact.
A counter-surveillance detection kit: patterns to look for, timing intervals for route surveillance identification, flush-point selection, and emergency abort protocols.
A fiber optic cable kit for under-door video reconnaissance — flexible, camera at the tip, six-meter reach — designed to fit in a glasses case.
A forged entry stamp for a country's border system, matched to a specific border crossing's current equipment, with a two-year validity period.
A burn device — a modified book — that ignites its contents from within on a timed delay, producing a fire hot enough to destroy documents but contained enough not to trigger building alarms.
A personal encryption suite on a phone that runs no visible applications, communicates over standard data connections, and is indistinguishable from an ordinary consumer handset.
A vehicle tail kit: three sets of disguise elements — hat, jacket, glasses — staged in a bag for quick changes during mobile surveillance or counter-surveillance.
A threat assessment report on a target location: entry points, guard patterns, camera positions, response times, and a recommended approach window — prepared for a specific facility.
A concealed recording device in a pair of glasses that captures video at 1080p for three hours, with a wireless transfer option for live monitoring.
A multi-spectrum analysis tool that detects infrared beams, laser trip wires, and pressure-sensitive floor systems when swept through a room.
An operational support fund in a specific denomination and currency, banded in a standard bank wrap, totaling a figure that would not attract comment at most borders.
A social media monitoring package for a specific target — aggregated profiles, relationship maps, location patterns, behavioral triggers — formatted for operational use.
A concealed satellite phone with a collapsible antenna, designed to look like an external battery when closed, with a specific emergency frequency pre-programmed.
A classified technical report on a foreign surveillance system — capabilities, blind spots, detection thresholds — that should not be outside a secure facility.
A seismic microphone designed to be placed against a wall or floor and capture audio from an adjacent space without physical access to that space.
A covert entry log — a record of physical access to a secure facility over an eighteen-month period — showing a pattern that whoever compiled it found significant.
A disguised explosive device in a standard electrical outlet adapter — triggered remotely — designed to produce structural damage to a specific type of server room.
A mobile surveillance platform — a modified van — with darkened windows, antenna concealment, independent power, and twelve hours of onboard recording capability.
A set of challenge and response codes for a specific network of assets, formatted as a personal contact list with built-in date-offset encryption.
A portable polygraph kit — not agency-grade, field-expedient — with a reference guide for interpretation and a specific protocol for field recruitment vetting.
A cover story package for a specific country: backstory, family history, employment records, and a prepared answer for the twenty most common entry interview questions.
A modified hotel key card that, when held against a standard electronic lock, captures the access credential and allows re-entry within a six-hour window.
A personal protection detail avoidance guide for a specific public figure — patterns, schedule habits, handover locations, blind spots — compiled from open-source surveillance.
A micro-film reader — functional, compact — for a format that was standard thirty years ago and is now found primarily in archive facilities that haven't updated their equipment.
A dead hand device that sends a pre-written message to three recipients if not reset within a specific time window each day.
A concealed compartment in a shoe heel, waterproofed, sized for a USB drive and four folded document pages.
A radio frequency identification reader disguised as a phone case that captures RFID data from access cards, credit cards, and passports within a thirty-centimeter range.
An emergency exfiltration package: false documents, emergency currency, a contact address, a route map, and a single-use communication device, sealed in a tamper-evident envelope.
A behavioral analysis report on a meeting counterpart — body language tells, deception patterns, pressure responses, rapport triggers — prepared by a professional profiler.
A concealed audio device in a power adapter that activates on voice detection and transmits to a receiver up to five hundred meters away.
A modified ink cartridge that, when installed in a specific model of office printer, captures and transmits a copy of every document printed.
A field-expedient disguise kit: three complete changes of appearance — different hair, glasses, clothing — designed for serial use during a single surveillance operation.
A contact protocol for a network of accommodation addresses — mail drops — in six cities, with receiving contact names and the response timing that signals whether the drop is clean.
A concealed ceramic knife — non-metallic, passes standard security equipment — designed for close-quarters use and stored in a modified belt buckle.
A phone with a specific set of installed apps that allows access to a covert communication infrastructure while appearing to be a standard consumer handset with a legitimate digital history.
A concealed currency transfer mechanism — a modified briefcase lining — carrying value in a form that does not appear on standard financial surveillance systems.
A door alarm bypass device that maintains an alarm circuit while the door is physically opened, allowing covert entry without triggering the alarm.
An operational planning notebook in a personal cipher, detailing a multi-phase operation, with three of the planned phases already completed and annotated.
A portable microwave transmission device that can send large data files in a directed beam to a receiver up to eight kilometers away, invisible to standard radio frequency monitoring.
A set of diplomatic credentials for a non-existing organization — complete, official-appearing, with a number that queries correctly against a database that was briefly compromised.
A concealed thermal camera in an ordinary briefcase that can map the heat signatures of electronic equipment through a standard interior wall.
A water-activated document destruction kit: thirty sheets of treated paper that become unreadable within three seconds of contact with any water-based liquid.
A laser microphone kit that converts window glass vibrations into audio, allowing conversation interception from up to three hundred meters without physical access to the space.
A concealed identity verification bypass — a thin film overlay for biometric scanners — that passes a specific enrolled identity rather than the person using it.

A communications device that broadcasts on a frequency used exclusively by a specific foreign intelligence service's field communications, using protocol patterns consistent with their infrastructure.
An autonomous surveillance drone, palm-sized, with four hours of flight time, optical and thermal imaging, encrypted telemetry, and a self-destruct on capture.
A personal archive: encrypted, on a physically unremarkable device, containing thirty years of an operative's operational history, contacts, methods, and assessments that no official record contains.

A pharmaceutical compound that, administered in a specific dosage over three days, produces medically documented symptoms of a specific illness without causing the illness, providing verifiable cover for an operative's absence.

A complete technical specification for a backdoor in a widely-deployed encryption standard — hardware-level, undetected, capable of retroactive decryption — in a format that suggests whoever wrote it was the one who put it there.
